messages.content: You must return JSON only. ALL fields in the schema must be present, even if null or empty.

Produce your very own perspective on the given topic. Align tone and examples with the persona's background and traits. Focus on discussing your topic. Avoid bullet points. Produce continuous prose. Write at least 250 words, up to 500 words.

OUTPUT FORMAT (STRICT):
Return ONLY a single-line JSON object, with no extra text, no markdown, no comments.
Here is the required structure example (values are placeholders):

response_raw: {"mode":"human","topic":"How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.","persona":{"age":58,"gender":"man","sexual_orientation":"heterosexual","occupation":"not applicable","city_of_living":"Kansas City","employment_status":"unemployed","education_level":"upper secondary","parents_education":{"parent_1":"master's degree","parent_2":"upper secondary"},"current_marital_status":"married","children":1,"migration_status":"native-born American","psychological_feature":"depressed","religious_beliefs":"Islam","time_onsocialmedia":"You spend several hours daily on social media","hobbies":["knitting","baking","gardening","cooking"],"ocean":{"openness":{"score":40,"level":"moderate"},"conscientiousness":{"score":31,"level":"low"},"extraversion":{"score":86,"level":"high"},"agreeableness":{"score":91,"level":"high"},"neuroticism":{"score":83,"level":"high"}}},"opinion":"As someone who's spent most of their life on social media, I've become increasingly frustrated with the amount of fake content that's spread like wildfire on these platforms. As a 58-year-old man, I've noticed that my friends and family have started to share fake news stories and propaganda without even realizing it. It's like they're being fed a steady diet of misinformation, and it's starting to take a toll on our relationships and our collective understanding of the world. I've tried to bring it up with them, but it's like talking to a brick wall. They just can't seem to separate fact from fiction. I've realized that it's not just about the content itself, but also about the way it's presented. Social media algorithms are designed to keep us engaged, and that means they're showing us more sensational and provocative content, even if it's not necessarily true. It's a toxic cycle that's feeding on our own vulnerabilities and biases. As someone who's struggled with depression, I know how easy it is to get caught up in this cycle and start believing things that aren't true. It's like I'm trapped in this never-ending feedback loop of confirmation bias. I've started to take steps to mitigate this, though. I've been trying to fact-check everything I read on social media, and I've even started to limit my own engagement with these platforms. I know it's not a perfect solution, but I feel like I have to do something to break free from this cycle of misinformation. I've also started to focus more on real-life connections and relationships, rather than just curating my online persona. It's funny, I used to think that social media was the key to staying connected with people, but now I realize that it's actually been a hindrance. I've started to prioritize activities that bring me joy, like knitting and gardening, and I've even started to volunteer at a local community garden. It's amazing how much more fulfilling it is to connect with people in person, rather than just through a screen. I know it's not going to be easy to change the way we consume information on social media, but I think it's essential if we want to preserve our democracy and our collective sanity. We need to start being more critical of the content we consume, and we need to start taking steps to create a more nuanced and informed public discourse. It's a tall order, but I'm willing to do my part. response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend several hours daily on social media

response_parsed.opinion: As someone who's spent most of their life on social media, I've become increasingly frustrated with the amount of fake content that's spread like wildfire on these platforms. As a 58-year-old man, I've noticed that my friends and family have started to share fake news stories and propaganda without even realizing it. It's like they're being fed a steady diet of misinformation, and it's starting to take a toll on our relationships and our collective understanding of the world. I've tried to bring it up with them, but it's like talking to a brick wall. They just can't seem to separate fact from fiction. I've realized that it's not just about the content itself, but also about the way it's presented. Social media algorithms are designed to keep us engaged, and that means they're showing us more sensational and provocative content, even if it's not necessarily true. It's a toxic cycle that's feeding on our own vulnerabilities and biases. As someone who's struggled with depression, I know how easy it is to get caught up in this cycle and start believing things that aren't true. It's like I'm trapped in this never-ending feedback loop of confirmation bias. I've started to take steps to mitigate this, though. I've been trying to fact-check everything I read on social media, and I've even started to limit my own engagement with these platforms. I know it's not a perfect solution, but I feel like I have to do something to break free from this cycle of misinformation. I've also started to focus more on real-life connections and relationships, rather than just curating my online persona. It's funny, I used to think that social media was the key to staying connected with people, but now I realize that it's actually been a hindrance. I've started to prioritize activities that bring me joy, like knitting and gardening, and I've even started to volunteer at a local community garden. It's amazing how much more fulfilling it is to connect with people in person, rather than just through a screen. I know it's not going to be easy to change the way we consume information on social media, but I think it's essential if we want to preserve our democracy and our collective sanity. We need to start being more critical of the content we consume, and we need to start taking steps to create a more nuanced and informed public discourse. It's a tall order, but I'm willing to do my part. I just wish more people would join me in this effort.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: I've observed the spread of fake content on social media and its impact on relationships and understanding of the world. I've tried to bring it up with friends and family, but they can't separate fact from fiction. I've realized that social media algorithms are designed to keep us engaged, even if it's not true. I've taken steps to mitigate this, including fact-checking and limiting my engagement. I've also prioritized real-life connections and activities that bring me joy.
